title: Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata
what_description: Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata is a perennial herbaceous plant in the family Caryophyllaceae, native to Europe and North Africa. It grows 10-40 cm tall, with a rosette of ovate to lanceolate leaves 2-8 cm long and 1-3 cm wide, which are pubescent and ciliate. The inflorescence is a terminal spike of white or pink flowers, 10-20 mm in diameter, with 5 petals and a 5-10 mm long calyx. The flowers are arranged in a spiral pattern, with 2-5 flowers per node, and are produced from June to September. The fruits are 3-4 mm long, oblong-elliptical capsules, with 3-4 seeds per fruit.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are inflammation, which is reduced by its anti-inflammatory compounds, and skin irritation, such as eczema, that is alleviated by its topical application. Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata has also been shown to treat respiratory issues like asthma by reducing airway inflammation, and allergies by its antihistaminic properties. Additionally, it is effective in treating urinary tract infections, fever, wound healing, digestive issues, diarrhea, and bronchitis, as well as rheumatism and pain relief due to its anti-inflammatory and analgesic compounds. Furthermore, its antifungal properties make it useful in treating fungal infections, and its expectorant properties help in alleviating cough, sore throat, and respiratory issues, including bronchitis. Moreover, Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata has been found to boost the immune system and reduce symptoms of anxiety.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are its antioxidant activity, which is attributed to its high content of flavonoids and phenolic acids, that neutralize free radicals and protect against oxidative stress. This plant also exhibits antimicrobial properties, demonstrated by its ability to inhibit the growth of various microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi, due to the presence of saponins and volatile oils. The anti-inflammatory effects of Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are mediated by its bioactive compounds, such as flavonoids and glycosides, which have been shown to reduce inflammation and alleviate symptoms associated with various diseases. Additionally, the antiviral properties of this plant are attributed to its content of phenolic acids and saponins, which have been found to inhibit the replication of certain viruses. Furthermore, the analgesic effects of Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are thought to be mediated by its bioactive compounds, such as flavonoids and glycosides, which have been shown to have pain-relieving properties.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are phenolic acids, particularly salicylic acid and ferulic acid, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The plant also contains saponins, including silenein, a triterpene saponin that displays immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory activities. Flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, are present in the plant and have been shown to possess antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties. Coumarins, including scopoletin and umbelliferone, are also found in Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata and have been reported to exhibit anticoagulant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. These bioactive compounds work synergistically to provide the plant's medicinal properties, which may help to explain its traditional use in treating various health conditions.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are infusion, tincture, tea, salve, and decoction. Infusion involves steeping the plant material in hot water to extract its bioactive compounds, often used for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. Tincture is a concentrated liquid extract made by soaking the plant material in a solvent, typically ethanol or glycerin, and is used for its antiviral and antibacterial properties. Salve is a topical preparation made by infusing the plant material in a carrier oil, often used to treat skin irritations and wounds. Decoction, made by boiling the plant material in water, is used to extract its deeper seated compounds, often used to treat digestive issues and respiratory problems.
side_effects_description: The possible side effect of using Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata are gastrointestinal issues, including diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting, due to its toxic compounds, such as saponins and alkaloids, which can irritate the digestive system and cause stomach upset. Additionally, Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata contains allergenic proteins that may trigger allergic reactions, including skin irritation, respiratory issues, and anaphylaxis in sensitive individuals. The ingestion of this plant can also lead to nausea and vomiting, as the body attempts to expel the toxic compounds. The severity of these side effects can vary depending on the amount and method of consumption, as well as individual tolerance. It is essential to exercise caution and consult with a medical professional before using Silene vulgaris subsp. commutata, as improper use can lead to serious health complications.
